From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Surnames used by Han Chinese and Sinicized ethnic groups

**Chinese surnames** are used by [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") and [Sinicized](/wiki/Sinicize "Sinicize") ethnic groups in [Greater China](/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and among [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") communities around the world such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). [Chinese names](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name") begin with surnames, unlike the [Western tradition](/wiki/Western_name_order "Western name order") in which surnames are given last. Around 2,000 Han Chinese surnames are currently in use, but the great proportion of Han Chinese people use only a relatively small number of these surnames; 19 surnames are used by around half of the Han Chinese people, while 100 surnames are used by around 87% of the population.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) A report in 2019 gives the [most common Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames") as [Wang](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") and [Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)"), each shared by over 100 million people in China.[[3]](#cite_note-gbtimes-3) The remaining eight of the top ten most common Chinese surnames are [Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)"), [Liu](/wiki/Liu "Liu"), [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)"), [Yang](/wiki/Yang_(surname) "Yang (surname)"), [Huang](/wiki/Huang_(surname) "Huang (surname)"), [Zhao](/wiki/Zhao_(surname) "Zhao (surname)"), [Wu](/wiki/Wu_(surname) "Wu (surname)") and [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_(surname) "Zhou (surname)").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Two distinct types of Chinese surnames existed in ancient China, namely ***xing*** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [姓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%93 "wiktionary:姓"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *xìng*) ancestral clan names and ***shi*** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [氏](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B0%8F "wiktionary:氏"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *shì*) branch lineage names. Later, the two terms were used interchangeably, and in the present day, *xing* refers to the surname and *shi* may refer either the clan or maiden name. The two terms may also be used together as *xingshi* for family names or surnames. Most Chinese surnames (*xing*) in current use were originally *shi*. The earliest *xing* surname might be [matrilinear](/wiki/Matrilinear "Matrilinear"), but Han Chinese family name has been almost exclusively [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineality "Patrilineality") for a couple of millennia, passing from father to children. This system of patrilineal surnames is unusual in the world in its long period of continuity and depth of [written history](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history"), and Chinese people may view their surnames as part of their shared kinship and Han Chinese identity.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Women do not normally change their surnames upon marriage, except sometimes in places with more western influences such as [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). Traditionally Chinese surnames have been [exogamous](/wiki/Exogamous "Exogamous") in that people tend to marry those with different surnames.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The most common Chinese surnames were compiled in the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") work *[Hundred Family Surnames](/wiki/Hundred_Family_Surnames "Hundred Family Surnames")*, which lists over 400 names. The colloquial expressions *lǎobǎixìng* (老百姓; lit. "old hundred surnames") and *[bǎixìng](/wiki/Baixing "Baixing")* ([wikt:百姓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%99%BE%E5%A7%93 "wikt:百姓"), lit. "hundred surnames") are used in Chinese to mean "ordinary folks", "the people", or "[commoners](/wiki/Commoner "Commoner")".

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Chinese surnames have a history of over 3,000 years. Chinese mythology, however, reaches back further to the legendary figure [Fuxi](/wiki/Fuxi "Fuxi") (with the surname Feng), who was said to have established the system of Chinese surnames to distinguish different families and prevent marriage of people with the same family names.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Prior to the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") (fifth century BC), only the ruling families and the aristocratic elite had surnames. Historically there was a difference between ancestral clan names or *xing* ([姓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%93 "wikt:姓")) and branch lineage names or *shi* ([氏](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B0%8F "wikt:氏")). *Xing* may be the more ancient surname that referred to the ancestral tribe or clan, while *shi* denoted a branch of the tribe or clan. For example, the ancestors of the [Shang](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") had *[Zi](/wiki/Zi_(surname) "Zi (surname)")* (子) as *xing*, but the descendants were subdivided into numerous *shi* including [Yin](/wiki/Y%C4%ABn_(surname) "Yīn (surname)") (殷), [Song](/wiki/Song_(Chinese_surname) "Song (Chinese surname)") (宋), Kong (空), [Tong](/wiki/Tong_(surname) "Tong (surname)") (同) and others.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1) The distinction between the two began to be blurred by the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"). During the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), name usage was standardised, commoners started to acquire a surname or *xing*, and the *shi* also became *xing*.[[9]](#cite_note-looie-9) By the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), families only had *xing* or *xing-shi*. The great majority of Han Chinese surnames (now called *xing* or *xingshi*) that survive to modern times have their roots in *shi* rather than the ancient *xing*.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1)

In modern usage, *xing* is the surname, but the word *shi* survives as a word to refer to the clan. The term *shi* may be appended to the surname of a person; for example, a man with the Zhang surname may be referred to as Zhang-*shi* instead of his full name. It is used in particular for the paternal surname of a married woman, therefore in this case *shi* means [maiden name](/wiki/Maiden_name "Maiden name"), which a Chinese woman would continue to use after marriage.[[9]](#cite_note-looie-9)[[10]](#cite_note-scmp-10)

### *Xing*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Xing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fc/Radical_nuu_xing.png)](/wiki/File:Radical_nuu_xing.png)

The character for *xing* with the female radical 女 in red

The ancient *xing* were surnames held by the [noble clans](/wiki/Chinese_nobility "Chinese nobility"). They generally contain a "female" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 女; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *nǚ*) [radical](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_characters) "Radical (Chinese characters)"), for example [Ji](/wiki/Ji_Clan "Ji Clan") ([姬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%AC "wikt:姬")), [Jiang](/wiki/Jiang_(surname) "Jiang (surname)") ([姜](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%9C "wikt:姜")), [Yao](/wiki/Yao_(surname) "Yao (surname)") ([姚](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%9A "wikt:姚")) and [Yíng](/wiki/Y%C3%ADng "Yíng") ([嬴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AC%B4 "wikt:嬴")). This is taken as evidence that they originated from [matriarchal](/wiki/Matriarchal "Matriarchal") societies based on [maternal lineages](/wiki/Women_in_ancient_and_imperial_China#Neolithic "Women in ancient and imperial China"). The character for *xing* itself is composed of a female radical and the character for "give birth" (生, *shēng*).[[11]](#cite_note-11) *Xing* is believed to have been originally transmitted through women of noble birth, while noble men have *shi*.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1)

Scholars such as [Edwin G. Pulleyblank](/wiki/Edwin_G._Pulleyblank "Edwin G. Pulleyblank"), however, are unconvinced by the matriarchy theory of Chinese surnames due to a lack of independent evidence. An alternative hypothesis has been proposed, suggesting that the use of female radical in *xing* may have arisen from the clan [exogamy](/wiki/Exogamy "Exogamy") system used during the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (the words *xing* and *shi* also did not exist in the Shang dynasty [oracle bones](/wiki/Oracle_bones "Oracle bones")). In ancient times, people of the same *xing* were not permitted to marry each other and a woman married into an aristocratic clan needed to be of a different name.[[12]](#cite_note-pulleyblank-12) Based on observation of the evolution of characters in oracular scripture from the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") through the [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"): the 女 radical seems to appear during the Zhou period next to Shang [sinograms](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") indicating a clan or a tribe. This combination seems to designate specifically a female and could mean "lady of such or such clan". The structure of the *xing* sinogram could reflect the fact that in the royal court of Zhou, at least in the beginning, only females (wives married into the Zhou family from other clans) were called by their birth clan name, while the men were usually designated by their title or fief.

While people of the same *xing* were not permitted to marry each other, those with the same *shi* can. By the Han dynasty when everyone had *xing* and the surname was transmitted paternally, the practice continued, but it had changed to marriage between families of men on the paternal side being prohibited, but not on the maternal side.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1)

### *Shi*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Shi")]

Prior to the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (3rd century BC), China was largely a *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")* (feudal) society. As fiefdoms were divided and subdivided among descendants, so additional sub-surnames known as *shi* were created to distinguish between noble lineages according to seniority, though in theory they shared the same ancestor. In this way, a nobleman would hold a *shi* and a *xing*. *Xing*, however, was more important than *shi*.

The difference between *xing* and *shi* became blurred in the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") starting with women. For example: [Chunqiu](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_Annals "Spring and Autumn Annals") referred to Duke Xuan of [Lu](/wiki/Lu_(state) "Lu (state)")'s consort Lady Mujiang (穆姜), who bore the clan name (姓, *xing*) Jiang, as *Jiangshi* 姜氏, "[lady of the] Jiang *shi*" (!).[[12]](#cite_note-pulleyblank-12)

After the states of China were unified by [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") in 221 BC, surnames gradually spread to the lower classes. Most surnames that survive to the present day were originally *shi*.

### Origins of Chinese surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Origins of Chinese surnames")]

According to the chapter on surnames in the Han dynasty work *[Fengsu Tong](/wiki/Fengsu_Tongyi "Fengsu Tongyi") – Xingshi Pian* (風俗通姓氏篇), there are 9 origins of Chinese surnames: dynasty names, posthumous titles, ranks of nobility, state names, official positions, style names, places of residence, occupations, and events.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1) Modern scholars such as [Kiang Kang-Hu](/wiki/Jiang_Kanghu "Jiang Kanghu") proposed that there are 18 sources from which Chinese surnames may be derived,[[13]](#cite_note-13) while others suggested at least 24.[[14]](#cite_note-14) These may be names associated with a ruling dynasty such as the various titles and names of rulers, nobility and dynasty, or they may be place names of various territories, districts, towns, villages, and specific locations, the title of official posts or occupations, or names of objects, or they may be derived from the names of family members or clans, and in a few cases, names of contempt given by a ruler.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The following are some of the common sources:

1. ***Xing***: These were usually reserved for the central lineage of the ancient royal family, with collateral lineages taking their own *shi*. The traditional description was what were known as the "Eight Great *Xing*s of High Antiquity" ([上](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A "wikt:上")[古](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%A4 "wikt:古")[八](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%AB "wikt:八")[大](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7 "wikt:大")[姓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%93 "wikt:姓")), namely [Jiāng](/wiki/Ji%C4%81ng_(surname_%E5%A7%9C) "Jiāng (surname 姜)") ([姜](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%9C "wikt:姜")), [Jī](/wiki/J%C4%AB "Jī") ([姬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%AC "wikt:姬")), [Yáo](/wiki/Yao_(surname) "Yao (surname)") ([姚](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%9A "wikt:姚")), [Yíng](/wiki/Y%C3%ADng "Yíng") ([嬴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AC%B4 "wikt:嬴")), [Sì](/wiki/Si_(surname_%E5%A7%92) "Si (surname 姒)") ([姒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%92 "wikt:姒")), Yún ([妘](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A6%98 "wikt:妘")), [Guī](/wiki/Gui_(surname) "Gui (surname)") ([媯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AA%AF "wikt:媯")) and Rèn ([妊](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A6%8A "wikt:妊")), though some sources quote [Jí](/wiki/Ji_(surname_%E5%A7%9E) "Ji (surname 姞)") ([姞](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A7%9E "wikt:姞")) as the last one instead of Rèn. Of these *xing*, only Jiang and Yao have survived in their original form to modern days as frequently occurring surnames, while few others, like Ji, Yun and Ren, survived in forms without the female radical: 吉, 云 and 任.[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
2. **State name**: Many nobles and commoners took the name of their state, either to show their continuing allegiance or as a matter of national and ethnic identity. These are some of the most common Chinese surnames in the present day such as [Wú](/wiki/Wu_(surname) "Wu (surname)") ([吳](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%90%B3 "wikt:吳"), 9th most common), [Zhōu](/wiki/Zhou_(surname) "Zhou (surname)") ([周](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%91%A8 "wikt:周"), 10th most common)
3. **Name of a fief or place of origin**: Fiefdoms were often granted to collateral branches of the aristocracy and it was natural as part of the process of sub-surnaming for their names to be used. An example is Di, Marquis of Ouyang Village, whose descendants took the surname [Ouyang](/wiki/Ouyang_(surname) "Ouyang (surname)") ([歐陽](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AD%90%E9%99%BD "wikt:歐陽")). There are some two hundred examples of this identified, often of [two-character surnames](/wiki/Chinese_compound_surname "Chinese compound surname"), but few have survived to the present. Some families acquired their surname during the Han dynasty from the [Commandery](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)") they resided in.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
4. **Names of an ancestor**: Like the previous example, this was also a common origin with close to 500 or 600 examples, 200 of which are two-character surnames. Often an ancestor's [courtesy name](/wiki/Courtesy_name "Courtesy name") would be used. For example, [Yuan Taotu](/wiki/Yuan_Taotu "Yuan Taotu") took the second character of his grandfather's courtesy name Boyuan ([伯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BC%AF "wikt:伯")[爰](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%88%B0 "wikt:爰")) as his surname. Sometimes titles granted to ancestors could also be taken as surnames.
5. **Seniority within the family**: In ancient usage, the characters *[zhong](/wiki/Zhong_(surname)#Zhòng_仲 "Zhong (surname)")* ([仲](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%B2 "wikt:仲")), *shu* ([叔](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%94 "wikt:叔")) and *[ji](/wiki/Ji_(surname_%E5%AD%A3) "Ji (surname 季)")* ([季](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AD%A3 "wikt:季")) were used to denote the second, third and fourth (or last) eldest sons in a family. For the first son *[meng](/wiki/Meng_(surname) "Meng (surname)")* ([孟](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AD%9F "wikt:孟")) was meant for a child born to a secondary wife or a concubine, while *[bo](/wiki/Bo_(Chinese_surname)#柏_Bó "Bo (Chinese surname)")* [伯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BC%AF "wikt:伯") indicated a child born to the primary wife. These were sometimes adopted as surnames. Of these, Meng is the best known, being the surname of the philosopher [Mencius](/wiki/Mencius "Mencius").
6. **Official positions**, such as [Shǐ](/wiki/Sh%C7%90_(surname) "Shǐ (surname)") ([史](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B2 "wikt:史"), "historian"), [Jí](/wiki/Ji_(surname_%E7%B1%8D) "Ji (surname 籍)") ([籍](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%B1%8D "wikt:籍"), "royal librarian"), [Líng](/wiki/Ling_(surname) "Ling (surname)") ([凌](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%87%8C "wikt:凌"), "ice master"), Cāng ([倉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%80%89 "wikt:倉"), "granary manager"), Kù ([庫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BA%AB "wikt:庫"), "store manager"), Jiàn ([諫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%AB%AB "wikt:諫"), "[adviser](/wiki/Adviser "Adviser")"), Shàngguān ([上官](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E5%AE%98 "wikt:上官"), "high official"), [Tàishǐ](/wiki/Taishi_(surname) "Taishi (surname)") ([太史](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%AA%E5%8F%B2 "wikt:太史"), "grand historian"), Zhōngháng ([中行](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E8%A1%8C "wikt:中行"), "commander of middle [column](/wiki/Column_(formation) "Column (formation)")"), Yuèzhèng ([樂正](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%A8%82%E6%AD%A3 "wikt:樂正"), "chief musician"), and in the case of [Shang](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty")'s "Five Officials" ([五官](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E5%AE%98 "wikt:五官")), namely [Sīmǎ](/wiki/Sima_(Chinese_surname) "Sima (Chinese surname)") ([司馬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8%E9%A6%AC "wikt:司馬"), "minister of horses", akin to [defence minister](/wiki/Defence_minister "Defence minister"), almost of the exact same etymological origin of the English title/surname [Steward](/wiki/Steward "Steward")), [Sītú](/wiki/Situ_(surname) "Situ (surname)") ([司徒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8%E5%BE%92 "wikt:司徒"), "[minister of the masses](/wiki/Situ_(office) "Situ (office)")", akin to treasurer), Sīkōng ([司空](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8%E7%A9%BA "wikt:司空"), "minister of works", akin to minister of infrastructure), Sīshì ([司士](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8%E5%A3%AB "wikt:司士"), "minister of [yeomen](/wiki/Yeomen "Yeomen")", akin to chief [ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman")) and Sīkòu ([司寇](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8%E5%AF%87 "wikt:司寇"), "minister of bandits", akin to [attorney general](/wiki/Attorney_general "Attorney general"));
7. **General occupations**, as with [Táo](/wiki/Tao_(surname) "Tao (surname)") ([陶](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%B6 "wikt:陶"), "[potter](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery")"), [Tú](/wiki/Tu_(surname) "Tu (surname)") ([屠](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B1%A0 "wikt:屠"), "butcher"), [Bǔ](/wiki/Bu_(surname) "Bu (surname)") ([卜](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8D%9C "wikt:卜"), "[diviner](/wiki/Divination "Divination")"), Jiàng ([匠](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8C%A0 "wikt:匠"), "[craftsman](/wiki/Artisan "Artisan")"), [Wū](/wiki/Wu_(surname)#Wū_(巫) "Wu (surname)") ([巫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B7%AB "wikt:巫"), "[shaman](/wiki/Wu_(shaman) "Wu (shaman)")") and Chú ([廚](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BB%9A "wikt:廚"), "cook").
8. **Titles of nobility**, such as [Wáng](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") ([王](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8E%8B "wikt:王"), "king"), [Hóu](/wiki/Hou_(surname) "Hou (surname)") ([侯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BE%AF "wikt:侯"), "marquis"), [Xiàhóu](/wiki/Xiahou "Xiahou") ([夏侯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%8F%E4%BE%AF "wikt:夏侯"), "[Marquis](/wiki/Marquis "Marquis") of [Xia](/wiki/Xia_County "Xia County")") and [Gōngsūn](/wiki/Gongsun "Gongsun") ([公孫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%AC%E5%AD%AB "wikt:公孫"), "Duke's grandchild")
9. **Royal decree by the Emperor**, such as [Kuang](/wiki/Kuang_(surname) "Kuang (surname)") ([鄺](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%84%BA "wikt:鄺")), bestowed amongst other gifts to Kuang Yuping, previously Fang Yuping (方愈平), by [Emperor Xiaozong of Song](/wiki/Emperor_Xiaozong_of_Song "Emperor Xiaozong of Song"), upon making Yuping's daughter an imperial concubine.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
10. **Ethnic and religious groups**: [Non-Han Chinese peoples in China](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China") sometimes took the name of their ethnic groups as [sinicized](/wiki/Sinicization "Sinicization") surnames, such as [Hú](/wiki/Hu_(surname) "Hu (surname)") ([胡](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%83%A1 "wikt:胡"), "barbarian"), [Jīn](/wiki/Jin_(Chinese_surname) "Jin (Chinese surname)") ([金](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91 "wikt:金"), "[Jurchen](/wiki/Jurchen_people "Jurchen people")"), Mǎn ([滿](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BB%BF "wikt:滿"), "[Manchu](/wiki/Manchu "Manchu")"), [Dí](/wiki/Di_(surname) "Di (surname)") ([狄](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8B%84 "wikt:狄"), "[Di people](/wiki/Beidi "Beidi")"), Huí ([回](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9B%9E "wikt:回"), "[Hui people](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people")") and [Mùróng](/wiki/Murong "Murong") ([慕容](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%85%95%E5%AE%B9 "wikt:慕容"), a [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei") tribe). Many [Hui Muslims](/wiki/Hui_Muslim "Hui Muslim") adopted the [surname Ma](/wiki/Ma_(surname) "Ma (surname)") ([馬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%A6%AC "wikt:馬")), an old Chinese surname, when they were required to use Chinese surnames during the Ming dynasty as it sounded close to the first syllable of [Mohammad](/wiki/Mohammad "Mohammad"); it was also fitting for some of those who were caravaneers as the word means "horse".[[18]](#cite_note-18)
11. **Famous people:** A couple of surnames originate from famous people in Chinese history. For example, the surname 李 originates from [Lao Tzu.](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi") This probably means that people today with the surname 李 are mostly descendants of Lao Tzu, including the Tang emperors[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Chinese_surname#On_the_possible_origins_of_Li_(李) "Talk:Chinese surname")*].

Many also changed their surnames throughout history for a number of reasons.

* A ruler may bestow his own surname on those he considered to have given outstanding service to him; for example, the surname [Liu](/wiki/Liu "Liu") ([劉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8A%89 "wikt:劉")) was granted by emperors in the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), [Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)") ([李](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9D%8E "wikt:李")) during the Tang dynasty, and [Zhao](/wiki/Zhao_(surname) "Zhao (surname)") ([趙](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%B6%99 "wikt:趙")) from the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty").
* Others, however, may avoid using the name of a ruler, for example Shi ([師](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B8%AB "wikt:師")) was changed to [Shuai](/wiki/Shuai "Shuai") ([帥](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B8%A5 "wikt:帥")) to [avoid conflict](/wiki/Naming_taboo "Naming taboo") with the name of [Sima Shi](/wiki/Sima_Shi "Sima Shi"). Others may modify their name in order to escape from their enemies at times of turmoil, for example Duanmu ([端木](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%AB%AF%E6%9C%A8 "wikt:端木")) to Mu ([木](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%A8 "wikt:木") and [沐](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B2%90 "wikt:沐")), and Gong ([共](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%B1 "wikt:共")) to [Gong](/wiki/Gong_(surname) "Gong (surname)") ([龔](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BE%94 "wikt:龔")).
* The name may also be changed by simplification of the writing, e.g. Mu ([幕](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B9%95 "wikt:幕")) to [Mo](/wiki/Mo_(Chinese_surname) "Mo (Chinese surname)") ([莫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8E%AB "wikt:莫")), or reducing from double or multiple character names to single character names, e.g. Duangan ([段干](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AE%B5%E5%B9%B2 "wikt:段干")) to Duan ([段](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AE%B5 "wikt:段")).
* It may also have occurred through error, or changed due to a dissatisfaction with the name (e.g. Ai [哀](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%93%80 "wikt:哀"), "sorrow", to Zhong [衷](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A1%B7 "wikt:衷"), "heartfelt feeling").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Usage")]

Chinese surnames or family names are written *before* the first name or [given name](/wiki/Chinese_given_name "Chinese given name").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Therefore, someone named Wei ([伟](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BC%9F "wikt:伟")) from the [Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)") ([张](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%A0 "wikt:张")) family is called "Zhang Wei" ([张伟](/wiki/Zhang_Wei_(disambiguation) "Zhang Wei (disambiguation)")) and not "Wei Zhang". Chinese women generally retain their maiden name and use their name unchanged after marriage, but in modern times in some communities, some women may choose to attach their husband's surname to the front.[[21]](#cite_note-fbiic-21) Chinese surname is [patrilinear](/wiki/Patrilinear "Patrilinear") where the father's surname is passed on to his children, but more recently some people have opted to use both parents' surnames; although this practice has increased in recent times, it is still relatively uncommon in China, with those who adopted both parents' surnames numbering at only 1.1 million in 2018 (up from 118,000 in 1990).[[3]](#cite_note-gbtimes-3) Adoption of matrilineal surnames is known to have been occurred historically; in Taiwan, some matrilineal surnames may have arose due to occupation by Japan where such practice was more common.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Some Chinese outside of mainland China, particularly those from the Chinese immigrant communities around the world and those who have acquired a Christian or Western first name, have adopted the Western convention when giving their name in English, placing their surname last. Examples of those commonly known in the West include [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan") (Chinese name Chan Kong-sang), [Jimmy Choo](/wiki/Jimmy_Choo "Jimmy Choo") (Chinese name Choo Yeang Keat), and [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma"). Those with a Western first name can write their name in English in various ways – some may add the Western first name in front and the Chinese given name last (the surname is therefore in the middle), or fully Westernised with both the Western and Chinese given names before the Chinese surname.[[21]](#cite_note-fbiic-21) Examples include [Carrie Lam](/wiki/Carrie_Lam "Carrie Lam"), originally named Cheng Yuet-ngor (Cheng is the surname), but who has acquired her husband's surname Lam and a Western first name as Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor.

Due to the different spelling conventions and dialects as well as the different spelling preferences in the various countries these Chinese find themselves in, many people of the same Chinese surname can appear differently when written in English, for example the [Lin surname](/wiki/Lin_(surname) "Lin (surname)") (林) may also appear as Lam ([Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")) or Lim ([Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien")). Some Chinese surnames that appear to be the same written in English may also be different in Chinese due to different characters having the same or similar pronunciations, dialectal differences, or non-standard romanizations (see section on variation in romanization below).[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-oxford-24)

Surnames are not evenly distributed throughout China's geography. In northern China, Wáng ([王](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8E%8B#Chinese "wiktionary:王")) is the most common surname, being shared by 9.9% of the population. Next are Lǐ ([李](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9D%8E#Chinese "wiktionary:李")), Zhāng ([张](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%A0#Chinese "wiktionary:张")/[張](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%B5#Chinese "wiktionary:張")) and Liú ([刘](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%88%98#Chinese "wiktionary:刘")/[劉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8A%89#Chinese "wiktionary:劉")). In the south, Chén ([陈](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%88#Chinese "wiktionary:陈")/[陳](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%B3#Chinese "wiktionary:陳")) is the most common, being shared by 10.6% of the population. Next are Lǐ (李), Huáng ([黄](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BB%84#Chinese "wiktionary:黄")/[黃](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BB%83#Chinese "wiktionary:黃")), Lín ([林](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9E%97#Chinese "wiktionary:林")) and Zhāng (张/張). Around the major crossing points of the [Yangzi River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), the most common surname is Lĭ (李), taking up 7.7%, followed by Wáng (王), Zhāng (张/張), Chan/Chén (陈/陳) and Liú (刘/劉).

A 1987 study showed over 450 family names in common use in Beijing, but there were fewer than 300 family names in [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Furthermore, a 2012 study found that there was the lowest amount of [isonymy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/isonymy "wikt:isonymy") in surnames among the population around middle and lower reaches of [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") both on the provincial and county levels.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Additionally, it was found that counties with the highest values of isonymy were distributed in the provinces with high proportions of ethnic minorities. According to the [dendrogram](/wiki/Dendrogram "Dendrogram") of surname distances, several clusters could be identified. Most provinces in a cluster were [conterminous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/coterminous "wikt:coterminous") with one another. The one exception to this pattern could be explained by [demic](/wiki/Demic_diffusion "Demic diffusion") migration observed where the Han Chinese migrated to [Northeastern China](/wiki/Northeastern_China "Northeastern China").

A study by [geneticist](/wiki/Geneticist "Geneticist") [Yuan Yida](/wiki/Yuan_Yida "Yuan Yida") has found that of all the people with a particular surname, there tends to be a population concentration in a certain province,[[26]](#cite_note-26) as tabulated to the right. It does not show, however, the most common surnames in any one province.

The 55th most common family name "Xiào" ([肖](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%82%96#Chinese "wiktionary:肖")) appears to be very rare in Hong Kong. This is explained by the fact Hong Kong uses [traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_character "Traditional Chinese character") rather than [simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_character "Simplified Chinese character"). Originally, the surname [蕭](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%95%AD#Chinese "wiktionary:蕭") (Xiāo) was rather common while the surname 肖 (Xiào) was extremely rare, if not non-existent (it is mentioned only sporadically in historical texts). The first round of simplification in 1956 simplified 蕭 into [萧](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%90%A7#Chinese "wiktionary:萧"), keeping 蕭/萧 and 肖 distinct. However the [second-round](/wiki/Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters "Second round of simplified Chinese characters") in 1977, which has long been abolished, merged 萧 and 肖 into 肖. Despite the retraction of the second round, some people have kept 肖 as their surname, so that there are now two separate surnames, 萧 and 肖.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Chén ([陈](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%88#Chinese "wiktionary:陈")/[陳](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%B3#Chinese "wiktionary:陳")) is perhaps the most common surname in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), where it is romanized as Chan. It is the [most common Chinese surname](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames#Singapore "List of common Chinese surnames") in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), where it is usually romanized as Tan, and is also common in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), where it is romanized as Chén.

Fāng ([方](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%96%B9#Chinese "wiktionary:方")), which is only the 47th most common overall, is much more common in San Francisco's Chinatown in the United States, although the surname is more often than not romanized as Fong, as based on the [Yue](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") dialect. As with the concentration of family names, this can also be explained statistically, as a person with an uncommon name moving to an unsettled area and leaving his family name to large number of descendants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), surname distributions in China largely settled down.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Kuàng](/wiki/Kuang_(surname) "Kuang (surname)") ([邝](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%82%9D#Chinese "wiktionary:邝")/[鄺](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%84%BA#Chinese "wiktionary:鄺")) family, for example, migrated from the northern capital and settled in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") after the Song dynasty revolts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Villages are often made up of a single [patrilineage](/wiki/Patrilineage "Patrilineage") with individuals having the same surname, often with a common male ancestor. They usually intermarry with others from nearby villages, creating genetic clusters.

## Surnames at present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Surnames at present")]

Of the thousands of surnames which have been identified from historical texts prior to the modern era, most have either been lost (see [extinction of family names](/wiki/Extinction_of_family_names "Extinction of family names")) or simplified. Historically there are close to 12,000 surnames recorded including those from non-Han Chinese ethnic groups, of which only about 3,100 are in current use,[[27]](#cite_note-27) a factor of almost 4:1 (about 75%) reduction. A 2019 figure however put the total number of Chinese family names at 6,150.[[3]](#cite_note-gbtimes-3) Of Han Chinese surnames, the largest number ever recorded was 6,363 (3,730 single-character surnames, 2,633 multiple-character surnames), around 2,000 of which are still in use.[[1]](#cite_note-du-1) Chinese Surname extinction is due to various factors, such as people taking the names of their rulers, orthographic simplifications, taboos against using characters from an emperor's name, and others. A recent example of near surname extinction is the rare surname Shan (𢒉).[[28]](#cite_note-shan-28) The character may not be displayed on computer systems used by government officials, and people born after the system change as well as people who want to avoid possible problems changed their name to another character such as Xian (冼). The name is still used by the older people, but some people from the village are concerned that future generations will forget their name origin.[[28]](#cite_note-shan-28)

While new names have arisen for various reasons, this has been outweighed by old names disappearing. The most significant factor affecting the surname frequency is other ethnic groups identifying as Han and adopting Han names.[[29]](#cite_note-du1992-29) In recent centuries some two-character surnames have often dropped a character. Since the founding of the People's Republic of China, moreover, some surnames have been graphically simplified.

Although there are thousands of Chinese family names, the [100](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames") most common, which together make up less than 5% of those in existence, are shared by 85% of the population. The three most common surnames in Mainland China are [Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)"), [Wang](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") and [Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)"), which make up 7.9%, 7.4% and 7.1% respectively. Together they number close to 300 million and are easily the most common surnames in the world. In Chinese, the phrase "third son of Zhang, fourth son of Li" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): [张](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%A0 "wikt:张")[三](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89 "wikt:三")[李](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9D%8E "wikt:李")[四](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9B%9B "wikt:四"); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhāng sān Lǐ sì*) is used to mean "just anybody".

In a 1990 study, the top 200 family names accounted for over 96% of a random sample of 174,900 persons, with over 500 other names accounting for the remaining 4%. In a different study (1987), which combined data from Taiwan and China (sample size of 570,000 persons), the top 19 names covered 55.6%,[[30]](#cite_note-30) and the top 100 names covered 87% of the sample. Other data suggest that the top 50 names comprise 70% of the population.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Most commonly occurring Chinese family names have only one character; however, about twenty [double-character family names](/wiki/Chinese_compound_surname "Chinese compound surname") have survived into modern times. These include [Sima](/wiki/Sima_(Chinese_surname) "Sima (Chinese surname)") ([司](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8 "wiktionary:司") [馬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%A6%AC "wiktionary:馬"), simp. [司](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8 "wiktionary:司") [马](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%A9%AC "wiktionary:马")), [Zhuge](/wiki/Zhuge "Zhuge") ([諸](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%AB%B8 "wiktionary:諸") [葛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%91%9B "wiktionary:葛"), simp. [诸](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%AF%B8 "wiktionary:诸") [葛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%91%9B "wiktionary:葛")), [Ouyang](/wiki/Ouyang "Ouyang") ([歐](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AD%90 "wiktionary:歐") [陽](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%99%BD "wiktionary:陽"), simp. [欧](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%AC%A7 "wiktionary:欧") [阳](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%98%B3 "wiktionary:阳")), occasionally romanized as *O'Young*, suggesting an Irish origin to English-speakers, and [Situ](/wiki/Situ_(surname) "Situ (surname)") (or Sito [司](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B8 "wiktionary:司") [徒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BE%92 "wiktionary:徒")). Sima, Zhuge, and Ouyang also happen to be the surnames of four well-known pre-modern Chinese historical figures. There are family names with three or more characters, but usually those are not ethnically Han Chinese. For example, Aixinjueluo ([愛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%84%9B "wiktionary:愛")[新](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%96%B0 "wiktionary:新")[覺](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A6%BA "wiktionary:覺")[羅](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%BE%85 "wiktionary:羅"), also romanized from the [Manchu language](/wiki/Manchu_language "Manchu language") as [Aisin Gioro](/wiki/Aisin_Gioro "Aisin Gioro")), was the family name of the [Manchu](/wiki/Manchu_people "Manchu people") royal family of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"). The longest recorded surname written using [hanzi](/wiki/Hanzi "Hanzi") characters is Lunalouyugumuzheshuduotumuku'adebu'axi ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 魯納婁于古母遮熟多吐母苦啊德補啊喜; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Lǔnàlóuyúgǔmǔzhēshúduōtǔmǔkǔ'ādébǔ'āxǐ*), an extremely rare surname reportedly used by members of the [Yi ethnic group](/wiki/Yi_people "Yi people") in [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") province, with seventeen characters in total.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Variations in romanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Variations in romanization")]

[Transliteration of Chinese](/wiki/Transliteration_of_Chinese "Transliteration of Chinese") family names (see [List of common Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames")) into foreign languages poses a number of problems. Chinese surnames are shared by people speaking a number of dialects and languages which often have different pronunciations of their surnames. The spread of the [Chinese diaspora](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") into all parts of the world resulted in the [Romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") of the surnames based on different languages and Chinese dialects. Countries that have adopted the system of Chinese surnames such as Vietnam and Korea also spell them according to their own pronunciations. As a result, it is common for the same surname to be [transcribed](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") differently. For example, the [Chen (陳) surname](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") can appear as Chan ([Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"), e.g. [Jackie Chan](/wiki/Jackie_Chan "Jackie Chan")), Tan ([Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien")), Tang ([Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect")), Chin ([Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") and [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language")), Trần ([Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language")) and others; the [Li (李) surname](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)") may appear as Lee (an example is [Lee Kuan Yew](/wiki/Lee_Kuan_Yew "Lee Kuan Yew")), the [Zhou (周) surname](/wiki/Zhou_(surname) "Zhou (surname)") can appear as Chou, Chew, Jew and many others (e.g. [Wakin Chau](/wiki/Wakin_Chau "Wakin Chau") and [Jimmy Choo](/wiki/Jimmy_Choo "Jimmy Choo")); while the [Zheng surname](/wiki/Zheng_(surname) "Zheng (surname)") (鄭/郑) can be romanized into Chang, Cheng, Chung, Teh, Tay, Tee, Tsang, Zeng or Zheng (in [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"), Chang, Cheng, Zheng and Zeng are all different names). In certain dialects, different surnames could be [homonyms](/wiki/Homonyms "Homonyms") so it is common for family names to appear ambiguous when transliterated. Translating Chinese surnames from foreign transliteration often presents ambiguity. For example, the surname "[Li](/wiki/List_of_surnames_romanized_Li "List of surnames romanized Li")" are all Mandarin-based pinyin transliteration for the surnames Lí ([黎](/wiki/L%C3%AD_(surname_%E9%BB%8E) "Lí (surname 黎)")); Lǐ ([李](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)"), 理 and 里); and Lì ([郦/酈](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E9%85%88) "Li (surname 酈)"), [栗](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%A0%97) "Li (surname 栗)"), [厉/厲](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E5%8E%B2) "Li (surname 厲)"), and [利](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E5%88%A9) "Li (surname 利)")) depending on the [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") which is usually omitted in foreign transliterations.[[24]](#cite_note-oxford-24)

Due to the different pronunciations and romanizations, it is sometimes easy to tell whether a Chinese person has origins in mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, or Taiwan. Generally, people of Mainland descent will have their surnames and names in [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"). Those from Taiwan use [Wade-Giles](/wiki/Wade-Giles "Wade-Giles") romanization. People from Southeast Asia (mainly Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines) and Hong Kong usually base their romanization of surnames and names on the [Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") and [Cantonese languages](/wiki/Cantonese_language "Cantonese language"). The younger generation from Singapore often has their surname in dialect (Hokkien, Teochew, Hainanese, Cantonese, and Hakka) and given names in English, Mandarin, or both.

Some people use non-standard romanizations, e.g. the Hong Kong [media mogul](/wiki/Media_mogul "Media mogul") 邵逸夫 [Run Run Shaw](/wiki/Run_Run_Shaw "Run Run Shaw")'s surname 邵 is spelt as Shaw (Shao in pinyin).

The use of different systems of romanization based on different Chinese language variants from 1900~1970 also contributed to the variations.

## Sociological use of surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sociological use of surnames")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/TongshanXian-G106-G316-junction-0018.jpg/250px-TongshanXian-G106-G316-junction-0018.jpg)](/wiki/File:TongshanXian-G106-G316-junction-0018.jpg)

Many village names in China are linked to surnames. Pictured is Jiajiayuan (贾家源), i.e. "[Jia Family](/wiki/Jia_(surname) "Jia (surname)")'s Spring", in Honggang Town, [Tongshan County, Hubei](/wiki/Tongshan_County,_Hubei "Tongshan County, Hubei")

Throughout most of Chinese history, surnames have served sociological functions. Because of their association with the aristocratic elite in their early developments, surnames were often used as symbols of nobility. Thus nobles would use their surnames to be able to trace their ancestry and compete for seniority in terms of hereditary rank. Examples of early [genealogies](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy") among the royalty can be found in [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian")'s *Historical Records*, which contain tables recording the descent lines of noble houses called *shibiao* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 世表; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *shìbiǎo*).

Later, during the Han dynasty, these tables were used by prominent families to glorify themselves and sometimes even to legitimize their political power. For example, [Cao Pi](/wiki/Cao_Pi "Cao Pi"), who forced the abdication of the last Han emperor in his favor, claimed descent from the [Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor"). Chinese emperors sometimes passed their own surnames to subjects as honors. Unlike European practice in which some surnames are obviously noble, Chinese emperors and members of the royal family had regular surnames except in cases where they came from non-Han ethnic groups. This was a result of Chinese imperial theory in which a commoner could receive the [Mandate of Heaven](/wiki/Mandate_of_Heaven "Mandate of Heaven") and become emperor. Upon becoming emperor, the emperor would retain his original surname. Also as a consequence, many people also had the same surname as the emperor, but had no direct relation to the royal family.

The Tang dynasty was the last period when the great aristocratic families, mostly descended from the nobility of pre-Qin states, held significant centralized and regional power. The surname was used as a source of prestige and common allegiance. During the period many genealogical records called *pudie* ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 谱牒; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 譜牒; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *pǔdié*) were compiled to trace the complex descent lines of families or clans and their marriage ties to other families or clans. Many of these were collected by [Ouyang Xiu](/wiki/Ouyang_Xiu "Ouyang Xiu") in his *New History of Tang*. To differentiate between different surnames, the Tang also choronyms before stating beforehand, for example Lǒngxī Lǐshì 隴西李氏, meaning Li of Longxi. These were generally the names of commanderies used prior to the reorganization during the Tang, so that they became exclusively associated to clans as their common use had died out. Cadet branches were also listed for further differentiation, such as Gūzāng Fáng 姑臧房, meaning Clan Li of Guzang.

During the Song dynasty, ordinary clans began to organize themselves into corporate units and produce genealogies. This trend was led by the poet [Su Shi](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi") and his father. As competition for resources and positions in the bureaucracy intensified, individuals used their common ancestry and surname to promote solidarity. They established schools to educate their sons and held common lands to aid disadvantaged families. Ancestral temples were also erected to promote surname identity. Clan cohesion was usually encouraged by successive imperial governments since it aided in social stability. During the Qing dynasty surname associations often undertook extrajudicial roles, providing primitive legal and social security functions. They played important roles in the Chinese diaspora to South-East Asia and elsewhere, providing the infrastructure for the establishment of trading networks. In southern China, however, clans sometimes engaged in armed conflict in competition for land. Clans continued the tradition of tracing their ancestry to the distant past as a matter of prestige. Most of these origin myths, though well established, are spurious.

As a result of the importance of surnames, rules and traditions regarding family and marriage grew increasingly complex. For example, in Taiwan, there is a clan with the so-called "double Liao" surname. The story is that "Chang Yuan-zih of Liao's in Siluo married the only daughter of Liao San-Jiou-Lang who had no son, and he took the oath that he should be in the name of Liao when alive and should be in the name of Chang after death."[[34]](#cite_note-34) In some places, there are additional taboos against marriage between people of the same surname, considered to be closely related. Conversely, in some areas, there are different clans with the same surname which are not considered to be related, but even in these cases surname [exogamy](/wiki/Exogamy "Exogamy") is generally practiced.

Surname identity and solidarity has declined markedly since the 1930s with the decline of [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") and later, the rise of [Communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") in Mainland China. During the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), surname culture was actively persecuted by the government with the destruction of ancestral temples and genealogies. Moreover, the influx of Western culture and forces of [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") have also contributed to erode the previous sociological uses of the Chinese surnames.

## Common Chinese surnames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Common Chinese surnames")]

### Mainland China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Mainland China")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of common Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames").

See also: [Sizes of the 20 largest Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_most_common_surnames_in_Asia#China "List of most common surnames in Asia") (2018)

According to a comprehensive survey of [residential permits](/wiki/Hukou_system "Hukou system") released by the [Chinese](/wiki/PRC "PRC") [Ministry of Public Security](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Security_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Public Security of the People's Republic of China") on 24 April 2007,[[35]](#cite_note-xinhua-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Eastday-36) the ten most common surnames in mainland China are [Wang](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") (王), [Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)") (李), [Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)") (张), [Liu](/wiki/Liu_(surname) "Liu (surname)") (刘), [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") (陈), [Yang](/wiki/Yang_(surname) "Yang (surname)") (杨), [Huang](/wiki/Huang_(surname) "Huang (surname)") (黄), [Zhao](/wiki/Zhao_(surname) "Zhao (surname)") (赵), [Wu](/wiki/Wu_(surname) "Wu (surname)") (吴), and [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_(surname) "Zhou (surname)") (周). The same names were also found (in slightly different orders) by a fairly comprehensive survey of 296 million people in 2006,[[37]](#cite_note-37) and by the [1982 census](/wiki/1982_Chinese_census "1982 Chinese census").[[38]](#cite_note-Peeps-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The top [100 surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames") cover 84.77% of China's population.[[36]](#cite_note-Eastday-36) The top 10 surnames each have populations greater than 20 million. The MPS survey revealed that the top 3 surnames in China have a combined population larger than Indonesia,[[40]](#cite_note-40) the world's fourth-most-populous country.

The 2019 report by Chinese Ministry of Public Security gives the surnames [Wang](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") and [Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)") as the most common ones, with each shared by over 100 million people in China. Each of the most common 23 surnames in China has more than 10 million users.[[3]](#cite_note-gbtimes-3)

A commonly cited fact from the 1990 edition of the *[Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records")* estimated that Zhang was the most common surname in the world,[[41]](#cite_note-41) but no comprehensive information from China was available at the time and more recent editions have omitted the claim.

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_surname&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

Main article: [List of common Taiwanese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Taiwanese_surnames "List of common Taiwanese surnames")

Distribution of Taiwanese surnames

1. 陳 Chen (11.1%)
2. 林 Lin (8.28%)
3. 黃 Huang (6.01%)
4. 張 Chang (5.26%)
5. 李 Lee (5.11%)
6. 王 Wang (4.12%)
7. 吳 Wu (4.04%)
8. 劉 Liu (3.17%)
9. 蔡 Tsai (2.91%)
10. 楊 Yang (2.66%)
11. Other (47.4%)

According to a comprehensive survey of residential permits released by the Taiwanese [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Taiwan) "Ministry of the Interior (Taiwan)")'s Department of Population in February 2005,[[42]](#cite_note-TaiRank-42) the ten most common surnames in Taiwan are [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") (陳), [Lin](/wiki/Lin_(surname) "Lin (surname)") (林), [Huang](/wiki/Huang_(surname) "Huang (surname)") (黃), [Chang or Zhang](/wiki/Zhang_(surname) "Zhang (surname)") (張), [Lee or Li](/wiki/Li_(surname_%E6%9D%8E) "Li (surname 李)") (李), [Wang](/wiki/Wang_(surname) "Wang (surname)") (王), [Wu](/wiki/Wu_(surname) "Wu (surname)") (吳), [Liu](/wiki/Liu_(surname) "Liu (surname)") (劉), [Tsai](/wiki/Cai_(surname) "Cai (surname)") (蔡), and [Yang](/wiki/Yang_(surname) "Yang (surname)") (楊).

Taiwanese surnames include some local variants like [Tu](/wiki/Tu_(surname) "Tu (surname)") (塗), which do not even appear among the *[Hundred Family Surnames](/wiki/Hundred_Family_Surnames "Hundred Family Surnames")*, as well as a number of recently created names that are fusions of various conventional surnames like Changchien (張簡) and Chiangfan (姜范). However, names in Taiwan show less diversity than China as a whole: the top ten comprise 52.63% of the Taiwanese population and the top hundred 96.11%.[[42]](#cite_note-TaiRank-42) There were also only 1,989 surnames recorded by the Ministry's survey,[[42]](#cite_note-TaiRank-42) against China's four or five thousand.

As is typical of China as a whole, these surnames conflate many different lineages and origins, although tradition may bind them to the same [ancestral temples](/wiki/Ancestral_temple "Ancestral temple") and rituals or [ban intermarriage](/wiki/Incest_taboo "Incest taboo"). For example, some Taiwanese converts to [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Taiwan "Presbyterian Church in Taiwan") adopted the name Kai (偕, [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") *Xié*) in honor of the Canadian missionary [George Leslie Mackay](/wiki/George_Leslie_Mackay "George Leslie Mackay") (馬偕, [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī") *Má-kai*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
